DEMURRER to COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jun-25-2021 Overruled
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES (VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE):
Atty. PETER ROLDAN, Esq. of Emergent LLP (Tel: 415-894-9284) and ALEXANDRA L. FOOTE, Esq. of Law Office of Alexandra L. Foote, P.C. (Tel: 415-271-0997) for the plaintiffs;
Atty. JOHN T. BROOKS, Esq. of Sheppard, Mullin, Richter & Hampton LLP (Tel: 619-338-6500) for defendant Crusader Insurance Company.

HEARING RE: DEFENDANT CRUSADER INSURANCE COMPANY'S DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant's demurrer to the complaint's class claims is overruled.

"The decision whether a case is suitable to proceed as a class action ordinarily is made on a motion for class certification." (Tucker, 208 Cal.App.4th at 211.) "A trial court may sustain a demurrer to class action allegations where it concludes as a matter of law that, assuming the truth of the factual allegations in the complaint, there is no reasonable possibility that the requirements for class certification will be satisfied." (Schermer v. Tatum (2016) 245 Cal.App.4th 912, 923.) "[A]ll that is normally required for a complaint to survive demurrers to the propriety of class litigation is that the complaint allege facts that tend to show: (1) an ascertainable class of plaintiffs, and (2) questions of law and fact which are common to the class." (Arce v. Kaiser Foundation Health Plan, Inc. (2010) 181 Cal.App.4th 471, 487.)

The complaint alleges an ascertainable class of plaintiffs. "[A] class [i]s ascertainable when it is defined in terms of objective characteristics and common transactional facts that make the ultimate identification of class members possible when that identification becomes necessary." (Noel v. Thrifty Payless, Inc. (2019) 7 Cal.5th 955, 980.) The complaint defines the class as bars and restaurants, that among other qualifications, "suffered a loss of insured property as a direct result of one or more Governmental Orders and the presence of the COVID-19 virus on the property." (Compl., 101.) Defendant does not show that ultimate identification would not be possible based on this definition.

Defendant does not show that there is no reasonable possibility that there are common questions of law and fact. Such common questions of law and fact are adequately pled at Compl. 106. Defendant's reliance on Basurco v. 21st Century Ins. Co. (2003) 108 Cal.App.4th 110 and Newell v. State Farm General Ins. Co. (2004) 118 Cal.App.4th 1094 is unpersuasive. While the court in Newell sustained demurrer on the basis of common questions, it did so in the context of property damage resulting from the 1994 Northridge earthquake. (Id., 118 Cal.App.4th at 1103.) The problems with earthquake damage there that "would involve an individual assessment of . . . property, the damage sustained and the actual claims practices employed" are different than the questions of law and fact here. (See id.)

Order signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer, Jr.; Clerk: Sean Kane; Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR No. 8600, Email: maria.torreano@gmail.com (302/RBU)
